frameworks.name: low-dimensional neural trajectories
frameworks.one_line: delay-period population activity confined to a low-dimensional manifold over time

frameworks.name: recurrent neural network trained with backpropagation
frameworks.one_line: trained RNNs reproduce the observed time-encoding and dimensionality

frameworks.name: sustained-activity working memory
frameworks.one_line: constant persistent firing as the substrate of delay memory

frameworks.name: reservoir / high-dimensional dynamics
frameworks.one_line: memory and timing via rich high-dimensional recurrent trajectories

core_question: What neural population mechanisms simultaneously support working memory and the encoding of elapsed time during delays?
key_assumptions: delay activity in nonhuman primates carries both memory and timing signals
dimensionality of neural trajectories is a meaningful constraint on mechanism
decodability of elapsed time indexes the presence of slow-timescale signals
manipulations: task relevance of timing information across four delay experiments
comparison of candidate mechanisms (sustained, reservoir, trained RNN)
cumulative dimensionality of population trajectory measured over the delay
key_findings: elapsed time decoded with high precision when timing is task-relevant, lower when irrelevant
neural trajectories during delays are consistently low-dimensional
linear ramping component of single-neuron firing drives the slow timescale that enables time decoding
interpretations: constant sustained-activity and high-dimensional reservoir models are ruled out as delay mechanisms
backprop-trained recurrent networks capture both the timing and low-dimensional structure
